claim: The Soviet collapse may reflect institutional fragmentation and nationalist mobilization rather than legitimacy collapse of the Marxist-Leninist sacred political order per se — many Soviet citizens remained ideologically committed even as republics seceded, suggesting the order retained partial legitimacy while its institutional vessel failed.
rationale: Survey data from the late Soviet period and post-Soviet nostalgia research suggest the sacred political order did not simply lose credibility uniformly; the collapse was also a function of elite defection, economic crisis, and nationalist institutional dynamics that are analytically separable from legitimacy per se.
